sections.header: Verses 1–9 — "There Will Be Terrible Times in the Last Days"

heb.paragraph: The adjective χαλεπός (chalepos) occurs only twice in the NT: here and in Matt 8:28 describing the Gadarene demoniacs as 'exceedingly fierce.' BDAG gives 'hard to bear, troublesome, dangerous.' The word conveys danger and violence, not mere inconvenience. The 'last days' language signals an eschatological framework: the moral deterioration described is both present reality and end-time prophecy.

The noun μόρφωσις (morphosis) denotes the outward form or appearance, in contrast to the inner reality. These people have the external shape of religion but have denied its transforming power. BDAG gives 'embodiment, formulation, outward form.' The distinction between form and power is the Pastorals' diagnostic for religious hypocrisy.

panels.ctx: The vice list in vv. 2–5 contains nineteen terms describing moral decay, arranged in a roughly chiastic pattern beginning and ending with self-love (φίλαυτοι) and pleasure-love (φιλήδονοι). The reference to people who 'creep into households and capture weak-willed women' (v. 6) provides a crucial window into the false teachers' methodology: they targeted domestic settings and vulnerable individuals. Jannes and Jambres (v. 8)—named nowhere in the OT but found in Jewish tradition (the Targums, CD 5:18)—are the Egyptian magicians who opposed Moses (Exod 7:11–12).

cross.note: Paul's earlier vice list describing the moral consequences of suppressing the truth provides a theological parallel to this eschatological catalogue.
The OT account of the Egyptian magicians who imitated Moses' signs provides the narrative behind the Jannes/Jambres reference.
Jude's parallel warning about scoffers in the last days who follow their own ungodly desires confirms the early Christian expectation of end-time moral decline.

notes.note: In the last days, people will be lovers of themselves, lovers of money, boastful, proud, abusive, disobedient, ungrateful, unholy—having a form of godliness but denying its power. Have nothing to do with such people.
They are the kind who worm their way into homes and gain control over gullible women loaded down with sins. These women are always learning but never able to acknowledge the truth.

As Jannes and Jambres opposed Moses, so these teachers oppose the truth. They are men of depraved minds, rejected regarding the faith. But they will not get very far because their folly will be clear to everyone.

Paul paints a devastating portrait of end-time humanity: selfish, greedy, boastful, and religiously fraudulent. The most dangerous are those who maintain the outward form of religion while emptying it of transforming power. Their tactic of targeting the vulnerable reveals both their cowardice and their cunning. Yet their folly will eventually be exposed, just as Jannes and Jambres' sorcery was revealed as counterfeit before the genuine power of God working through Moses.
The phrase 'form of godliness' (μόρφωσιν εὐσεβείας) distinguishes between religion's outward shape and its inward transforming power—a critical diagnostic throughout the Pastorals.
Jannes and Jambres are identified in Jewish tradition (Targum Pseudo-Jonathan, CD 5:17–19, various midrashim) as the Egyptian magicians of Exodus 7:11. Their names do not appear in the OT itself.

The vice list's nineteen terms form an escalating catalogue that begins with self-love (φίλαυτοι) and ends with pleasure-love (φιλήδονοι), creating an inclusio of misdirected love. The diagnostic in v. 5—form without power—is the Pastorals' signature for religious hypocrisy.

The household-infiltration language (εἰσδύνοντες, creeping in) describes a deliberate strategy targeting the domestic sphere—the locus of the house-church. The Jannes/Jambres comparison frames the false teachers as counterfeit miracle-workers whose deception will ultimately be exposed.

The eschatological vice list functions as both prophecy and social description: the 'last days' are not merely future but already impinging on the present. The household-infiltration strategy targeting women (v. 6) reveals the social dimension of the heresy: it exploits the domestic setting where women had relative autonomy and access to teaching. The Jannes/Jambres typology assures Timothy that opposition to truth is ancient, patterned, and ultimately self-defeating.

heb.paragraph: A compound adjective from θεός (God) and πνέω (to breathe), θεόπνευστος is a NT hapax that has become the foundational term for the doctrine of biblical inspiration. The word describes Scripture's origin rather than its effect: it is 'breathed out by God' rather than merely 'inspiring.' Whether the adjective is predicate ('all Scripture is God-breathed and useful') or attributive ('all God-breathed Scripture is useful') affects the scope of the claim, though the predicate reading is strongly preferred by most scholars and translations.

The adjective ἄρτιος (artios) denotes completeness or fitness for a task. BDAG gives 'complete, capable, proficient.' The cognate verb ἐξαρτίζω (exartizō, to equip fully) in v. 17 reinforces the meaning: Scripture renders the man of God thoroughly equipped for every good work. The emphasis is functional, not merely informational.

panels.ctx: Paul's autobiographical section (vv. 10–12) contrasts his own suffering-marked ministry with the false teachers' easy lives. The reference to persecutions at Antioch, Iconium, and Lystra (v. 11) recalls events from Paul's first missionary journey (Acts 13–14) that Timothy would have known firsthand, since he was from Lystra (Acts 16:1). The Scripture passage (vv. 14–17) is one of the most theologically significant in the entire Pauline corpus, providing the classic prooftext for the doctrine of biblical inspiration and the sufficiency of Scripture.

cross.note: Peter's parallel statement that prophecy came not by human will but from God as men spoke being carried along by the Holy Spirit complements Paul's 'God-breathed' language.
The psalmist's celebration of Torah as perfect, trustworthy, right, and radiant provides the OT foundation for Paul's affirmation of Scripture's comprehensive usefulness.
The persecutions at Antioch, Iconium, and Lystra are narrated in Acts and provide the historical referent for Paul's autobiographical reminder in v. 11.

notes.note: Timothy knows Paul's teaching, conduct, purpose, faith, patience, love, endurance, persecutions, and sufferings. Everyone who wants to live a godly life in Christ Jesus will be persecuted. This is not optional.
Evil men and impostors will go from bad to worse, deceiving and being deceived. The trajectory of false teaching is always downward.

Continue in what you have learned and become convinced of, because you know from whom you learned it and how from infancy you have known the Holy Scriptures, which are able to make you wise for salvation.

All Scripture is God-breathed and is useful for teaching, rebuking, correcting, and training in righteousness, so that the servant of God may be thoroughly equipped for every good work. This is the definitive statement on biblical inspiration and sufficiency.

Paul contrasts his own suffering-marked faithfulness with the false teachers' deception. Persecution is the normal experience of godly living in a fallen world. Those who promise an easy Christianity preach a false gospel.

Scripture is God-breathed—it proceeds from God's mouth, not from human invention. Its fourfold usefulness (teaching, rebuking, correcting, training) covers both doctrine and life, theory and practice. The purpose is not mere knowledge but thorough equipment for every good work. Scripture is sufficient for salvation and for sanctification.

The adjective θεόπνευστος is a NT hapax. The predicate reading ('all Scripture is God-breathed and useful') is preferred over the attributive ('all God-breathed Scripture is useful') because the latter would imply some Scripture is not God-breathed.
The 'sacred writings' (ἱερα γράμματα) known from childhood refers primarily to the OT Scriptures, since the NT canon was not yet established.

The autobiographical contrast (vv. 10–12) establishes Paul's life as the antithesis of the false teachers' behavior. The persecution catalogue specifically cites events Timothy witnessed personally in Lystra (Acts 14:19–20), strengthening the personal bond and the argument.

The θεόπνευστος declaration is the most important single word in the NT doctrine of inspiration. The compound's emphasis is on origin: Scripture is the product of God's creative breath, not mere human religious reflection. The fourfold usefulness (διδασκαλίαν/ἐλεγμόν/ἐπανόρθωσιν/παιδείαν) covers positive and negative, doctrinal and ethical functions. The telos is the ἄρτιος person: complete, competent, fully equipped.

The autobiographical section creates a stark contrast: Paul's life of costly faithfulness vs. the imposters' trajectory of escalating deception. The citation of specific persecutions Timothy witnessed (Antioch, Iconium, Lystra) appeals to shared memory as a basis for continued loyalty.

The Scripture passage crowns the letter's argument about the transmission of sound teaching. The 'sacred writings' known from childhood are the Jewish Scriptures taught by Lois and Eunice (1:5), now reread christologically as pointing to salvation in Christ. The θεόπνευστος claim grounds Scripture's authority in divine origin while specifying its function as equipping for 'every good work'—the Pastorals' consistent criterion of authentic faith.

rows.label: vv.1–5
rows.text: Vice list for the last days

lit.note: Chapter 3 moves from eschatological warning (vv. 1–9) through autobiographical contrast (vv. 10–13) to the letter’s theological climax: the God-breathed character of Scripture as the foundation for equipping the man of God (vv. 14–17).

themes.note: Sovereignty and Prophecy peak in the inspiration passage. Judgment dominates the eschatological vice list. Faith persists through Paul’s autobiographical example of endurance.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Timothy
ppl.role: Paul’s young protégé; co-author of six NT letters
ppl.text: Timothy is recruited at Lystra on the second journey (Acts 16:1–3) — the son of a Jewish mother and Greek father, circumcised by Paul to aid Jewish outreach. He becomes Paul’s most trusted emissary, sent to troubled churches when Paul cannot go. Paul twice calls him "my true son in the faith" and addresses two personal letters to him. He is co-author of six Pauline letters and appears at the end of Hebrews.

ppl.name: Mark
ppl.text: Mark is a key figure in Godlessness in the Last Days.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "In the last days, people will be lovers of themselves, lovers of money, boastful, proud, abusive, disobedient, ungrateful, unholy—having a form of godliness but denying its power. Have nothing to do w…"

rec.quote: "Paul paints a devastating portrait of end-time humanity: selfish, greedy, boastful, and religiously fraudulent. The most dangerous are those who maintain the outward form of religion while emptying it…"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The phrase 'form of godliness' (μόρφωσιν εὐσεβείας) distinguishes between religion's outward shape and its inward transforming power—a critical diagnostic throughout the Pastorals."
